content:
[Page 1]
ResearchMind AI Evaluation Paper
Abstract
This paper evaluates a retrieval augmented generation assistant for research workflows. The
system extracts text from uploaded PDF papers, splits content into semantic chunks, creates
embeddings, stores vectors, and answers user questions with citations.
Methodology
The prototype uses a FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL metadata storage, FAISS vector indexing,
and Gemini language models. Users authenticate, upload PDF files, create chat sessions, ask
questions, generate summaries, and compare multiple papers.
Findings
Citation grounded answers reduce hallucination risk by tying responses to retrieved chunks.
Study tools such as summaries, flashcards, and literature reviews help researchers review
papers faster.
Limitations
The quality depends on PDF text extraction, embedding quality, retrieval configuration, and the
availability of the external Gemini API.

[Page 1]
Second ResearchMind Evaluation Paper
Abstract
This paper focuses on multi-document analysis for AI research assistants. It examines how
comparing uploaded papers can help researchers identify shared methods, differences in
findings, and gaps for future work.
Methodology
The system stores multiple papers per user, retrieves relevant chunks from each document, and
asks a Gemini model to produce a comparison table and narrative synthesis.
Findings
Multi-paper comparison is useful when papers discuss related systems but differ in datasets,
methods, limitations, or evaluation criteria.
Limitations
Comparison quality depends on having enough well-extracted source text from each uploaded
paper and accurate retrieval across the vector index.
